result:
It occurs to me, somewhere between the second and third cup of coffee, that every object in my apartment is engaged in a quiet, centuries-long negotiation with gravity. The coffee table—an inherited slab of walnut that once belonged to a great-aunt who wore gloves to the supermarket—sags exactly 2.3 millimeters in the center, a curvature I measured with a smartphone app one sleepless night. The books on the lower shelf have slowly, politely, adapted to that sag, their spines accommodating the new geometry of disappointment. Even the dust seems to have given up on the idea of floating; it settles into the grain of the wood like a cat that has finally accepted that no one will ever open the bedroom door again.

I think about this micro-collapse while the kettle rebuilds its pressure for cup number three. Somewhere inside that stainless-steel chamber, water is remembering what it was like to be rain, to be river, to be cloud. It bucks against the walls, pushing vapor hotter than the surface of Venus into the narrow neck of the spout. I imagine each molecule carrying a tiny diary of its past lives: “Tuesday—was a glacier, froze a Viking’s beard. Wednesday—evaporated from a child’s forgotten juice box. Thursday—condensed on the window of a Tokyo commuter train, spelled out brief kanji of condensation that translated loosely to please notice me.” The whistle is the collective sigh of a trillion such memories, too small to be tragic, too numerous to be silent.

Outside, the city is learning a new choreography for the season. The plane trees have begun to practice their annual striptease, one leaf at a time, each flutter to the pavement a rehearsal for the final naked bow. A delivery cyclist slices through the new chill, his bright pink insulated bag brimming with someone else’s comfort: pad thai, maybe, or tom yum calibrated to the Scoville level of heartbreak. The wind pushes his front wheel a few degrees off true, and for a moment he is every protagonist who has ever had to correct course mid-sentence, mid-life, mid-pandemic. I watch him recalibrate, pedal, vanish around the corner where the bakery pumps out smells of cardamom and regret.

Regret, I’ve noticed, has a specific weight: 1.2 grams per cubic centimeter, slightly lighter than myrrh, slightly heavier than daylight. I keep some in a tiny glass vial on the windowsill, harvested from the space between “I should have” and “I thought I still had time.” When sunlight strikes it at 17:42, the room fills with the faint hue of 1997—grass stains, dial-up modems, the plastic backpack of a cousin who moved to Calgary and stopped sending postcards. The regret refracts the light into a narrow band that lands on the wall exactly where a photo of us used to hang. The nail hole is still there, a mouth that never learned to close.

Books, meanwhile, are quietly committing mutiny. The ones I haven’t opened in years press their fore-edges against the neighboring volumes, whispering plots to swap places so that when I next reach for Proust I’ll get instead a field guide to North American turtles. They want to teach me how to bask. How to grow a shell that can store entire solar systems of patience. How to retract gracefully when the neighborhood dogs of distraction come sniffing. At night I hear their slow, papery shuffle—like dry snow, like the sound of my mother turning newspaper pages while I pretended to sleep in 1988. If I opened the door quickly enough, I could catch them mid-conspiracy, but I never do. I pretend I don’t notice, because acknowledging their rebellion would require me to admit how many of them I will never read before I die, and that number has its own gravity, its own sag in the center.

The ceiling, too, is thinking. Hairline cracks have appeared since last winter, a miniature San Andreas where paint plates grind against one another with tectonic patience. I lie on the floor and watch them widen a micron per week, mapping fault lines for a quake that will never make headlines. In the plaster’s fissures I see the trajectory of my forties: hairline, waistline, timeline. Once I pressed my eye so close that a speck of cottage-white stucco detached and drifted down, landing on my lashes like a star that had grown tired of radiance. For a moment my vision became a planetarium of paint flakes, each fleck a deferred dream—learning tango, visiting Reykjavík, apologizing first—burning up in the atmosphere of a single blink.

Upstairs, the neighbor I’ve never met is teaching her parrot to speak in the accent of her dead father. I know because at 11:11 every night the bird rehearses stock phrases with an inflection that carries the humidity of a childhood kitchen: Don’t slam the screen door, peanut. Did you feed the cat? The syllables seep through the plaster, travel the copper pipes, emerge in my faucet so that when I brush my teeth I taste memory, metal, salt. Sometimes I answer out loud: The cat is fed, the door is gentle, I’m still here. The parrot listens, repeats, revises. It has begun to add new sentences I’ve never heard: The moon is a stamp that never sticks properly. Your kettle is boiling the sky. Regret is lighter if you warm it first.

At 3:03 a.m. the building achieves a momentary equilibrium: every droop, every crack, every whispering book aligns into a single held breath. The walls stop settling; the fridge ceases its hum; the elevator cable relaxes mid-shaft. Even the mice in the walls pause, paws frozen above the peanut butter I smeared on a bit of cardboard because eviction feels like betrayal. In that hush I understand that I am not living among objects but among verbs that have temporarily solidified: to sag, to remember, to whistle, to forgive. The quiet is so complete I can hear the moonlight land on the roof—soft as a returned letter, soft as the moment before a parrot decides whether or not to speak your name.

Then the kettle clicks off. The radiator clicks on. Gravity remembers its ancient grievances, and the apartment resumes its slow negotiation with the planet’s pull. I pour the third cup, noting how water refuses to be tragic, how it accepts the shape offered, steams, cools, disappears. Somewhere in the plumbing, the memory of Tuesday’s glacier merges with the thought of the Viking’s beard, merges with the condensation on the Tokyo window, merges with the tiny kanji that still, stubbornly, translate: notice me, notice me, notice me.

And I do. I notice the sag, the crack, the vial of regret glowing like early dusk. I notice the books plotting their gentle revolution, the parrot inventing new cosmologies, the cyclist rounding a corner that leads to someone else’s hunger. I notice that 1,000 words, like 1,000 cups of coffee, like 1,000 apologies, never quite fill the space they’re poured into, but the steam—rising, vanishing—carries the crucial message, written in a language neither lighter nor heavier than daylight: keep going, keep going, keep going.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
